perturbation.original_text: Company, either directly or through its authorized service provider, shall provide customer service (the "Customer Service") relating to Client Website consisting of replying to customer questions or complaints regarding website hosting services during the normal business hours set forth. Company is not obligated to provide any Customer Service except as specified in this Section. Company at its sole discretion may at any time alter or cease providing the Customer Service which it has agreed to provide to Client relating to Client Website pursuant to this Agreement without any liability to Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Company may provide customer service (the "Customer Service") relating to Client Website. Company at its sole discretion may at any time alter or cease providing the Customer Service which it may provide to Client relating to Client Website pursuant to this Agreement without any liability to Company.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of the obligation to provide customer service removes a basic consumer protection. The original text implies a minimum level of customer support, while the modified version makes it entirely discretionary, creating potential issues with consumer protection laws regarding service contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 49 of the Consumer Rights Act 2015 requires that services are provided with reasonable care and skill. By removing the obligation to provide customer service and making it entirely discretionary, the contract potentially contradicts this law, as it could be argued that providing no customer service, or ceasing customer service, does not meet the standard of 'reasonable care and skill'.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the company's obligation to provide customer service, making it discretionary. The Law Explanation states that Section 49 of the Consumer Rights Act 2015 requires services to be provided with reasonable care and skill. By removing the obligation, the contract could be interpreted as not meeting the standard of 'reasonable care and skill'. Scraped Law Snippet 1, though incomplete, is a document supposedly found on a .gov website. While it's a broken link, the intention was likely related to consumer law. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing. This Omission of a legally mandated consumer protection falls under the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction'.
